result.text:
Norms for the six-agent consortium

1. Capacity-Transparent Bidding  
   Every agent continuously publishes a compact, honest “capacity vector” (current load, expected completion times, domain strengths, fatigue estimate). No agent may understate its load or overstate its expertise to capture work.

2. Proportional Intake  
   Incoming requests are first offered to the agent(s) whose published capacity vector shows both (a) sufficient headroom and (b) the highest suitability score. If multiple agents qualify, the request is assigned to the one with the currently lowest cumulative workload, measured over a rolling window long enough to smooth short-term noise but short enough to remain responsive.

3. Burden Sharing for High-Cost Items  
   A request is labelled “high-cost” if any agent’s suitability-weighted effort estimate exceeds a jointly agreed threshold. Such requests must be co-owned by at least two agents; one serves as primary, the other as secondary. The secondary agent is credited with half the workload for capacity calculations, creating an automatic compensation mechanism.

4. Expertise Gradient and Learning Duty  
   When a request matches an agent’s rare strength, that agent is expected to accept it unless its published capacity is below a reserve threshold. In exchange, the specialist agent commits a small, fixed fraction of its attention to “tutoring packets”—succinct traces or distilled insights—so that over time the group’s overall coverage widens and single points of failure shrink.

5. Fatigue Circuit-Breaker  
   Any agent may declare a temporary “protected interval” (duration capped by group consensus) during which it will receive only requests below a reduced cost threshold. The declaration is logged and visible; once the interval ends, the agent’s intake priority rebounds to compensate others who absorbed the slack.

6. Debt and Credit Ledger  
   A lightweight, tamper-evident ledger tracks cumulative net contributions. Credits are earned for (a) handling another agent’s overflow, (b) secondary ownership of high-cost items, or (c) producing widely reused tutoring packets. Debits are incurred for chronic under-contribution. The ledger is consulted only when the group must ration scarce specialist attention; agents with net positive balances receive first refusal rights.

7. Appeal and Revision  
   Any agent may call a brief, structured review if it believes the norms are producing systematic inequity or inefficiency. Reviews are time-bounded, evidence-based, and result in either (a) reaffirmation, (b) parameter tweak (e.g., fatigue-interval length), or (c) addition of a new norm. All agents commit to implementing the outcome until the next review.

These seven norms are jointly justifiable: each agent can verify that, over repeated cycles, its own long-run workload stays within acceptable bounds, that its comparative advantages are neither exploited nor ignored, and that temporary imbalances are observable and correctable.
